document.title: Biological evaluation of medical devices — Part 10: Tests for irritation and skin sensitization

sourceSummary.oneLine: ISO 10993-10:2010 specifies procedures for assessing medical devices and constituent materials for irritation and skin sensitization potential.
sourceSummary.officialUse: Used as the basic document for selecting and conducting tests to evaluate irritation and dermal sensitization responses relevant to the safety of medical materials and devices.
keyPoints: Describes pretest considerations, in vivo irritation and sensitization procedures, and key factors for interpreting results.

Requires a step-wise approach including material characterization, literature review, consideration of in vitro tests, in vivo animal tests, and non-invasive human tests or clinical trials where appropriate.
Includes normative annexes for material preparation, special irritation tests, and human skin irritation testing.
States that predictive test results cannot stand alone and should be validated against other information sources.

entities.name: ISO

entities.name: ISO/TC 194
entities.evidence: ISO 10993-10 was prepared by Technical Committee ISO/TC 194, Biological evaluation of medical devices.

entities.name: IEC
entities.evidence: ISO collaborates closely with the International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC) on all matters of electrotechnical standardization.

entities.name: OECD
entities.evidence: This part of ISO 10993 is based on numerous standards and guidelines, including OECD Guidelines, U.S. Pharmacopoeia and the European Pharmacopoeia.

entities.evidence: This part of ISO 10993 is based on numerous standards and guidelines, including OECD Guidelines, U.S. Pharmacopoeia and the European Pharmacopoeia.

entities.evidence: This part of ISO 10993 is based on numerous standards and guidelines, including OECD Guidelines, U.S. Pharmacopoeia and the European Pharmacopoeia.

concepts.name: Irritation
concepts.evidence: localized non-specific inflammatory response to single, repeated or continuous application of a substance/material

concepts.name: Skin sensitization
concepts.evidence: immunologically mediated cutaneous reaction to a substance

concepts.name: Step-wise approach
concepts.evidence: This part of ISO 10993 requires a step-wise approach, which shall include one or more of the following

concepts.name: Animal irritation test
concepts.evidence: An assessment is made of the potential of the material under test to produce dermal irritation in a relevant animal model.

concepts.name: Animal intracutaneous reactivity test
concepts.evidence: For medical devices that are used as an implant, the use of an intracutaneous (intradermal) reactivity test is indicated.

concepts.name: Human skin irritation test
concepts.evidence: This part of ISO 10993 allows skin irritation data to be obtained directly from humans for purposes of hazard identification.

concepts.name: Murine Local Lymph Node Assay
concepts.evidence: The murine Local Lymph Node Assay (LLNA) was internationally accepted for testing single chemicals as a stand-alone alternative to the guinea pig assays, and is now the preferred assay for chemicals.

concepts.name: Guinea Pig Maximization Test
concepts.evidence: So far, the two most commonly used methods for testing for skin sensitization are the Guinea Pig Maximization Test (GPMT) and the closed-patch test (Buehler test).

concepts.name: Closed-patch test
concepts.evidence: So far, the two most commonly used methods for testing for skin sensitization are the Guinea Pig Maximization Test (GPMT) and the closed-patch test (Buehler test).

concepts.name: Primary irritation index
concepts.evidence: For single exposure tests, determine the primary irritation index (PII) as follows.

evidence.claim: ISO 10993-10:2010 is an International Standard titled Biological evaluation of medical devices — Part 10: Tests for irritation and skin sensitization.
evidence.evidenceText: INTERNATIONAL STANDARD ISO 10993-10 Third edition 2010-08-01 Biological evaluation of medical devices — Part 10: Tests for irritation and skin sensitization

evidence.claim: The document describes procedures for assessing medical devices and constituent materials for irritation and skin sensitization potential.
evidence.evidenceText: This part of ISO 10993 describes the procedure for the assessment of medical devices and their constituent materials with regard to their potential to produce irritation and skin sensitization.

evidence.claim: The standard includes pretest considerations, in vivo test procedures, and key factors for interpretation of results.
evidence.evidenceText: This part of ISO 10993 includes: a) pretest considerations for irritation, including in silico and in vitro methods for dermal exposure; b) details of in vivo (irritation and sensitization) test procedures; c) key factors for the interpretation of the results.

evidence.claim: ISO 10993-10:2010 was prepared by ISO/TC 194 and replaces ISO 10993-10:2002.
evidence.evidenceText: ISO 10993-10 was prepared by Technical Committee ISO/TC 194, Biological evaluation of medical devices. This third edition cancels and replaces the second edition (ISO 10993-10:2002), which has been technically revised.

evidence.claim: The standard is intended as a basic document for selecting and conducting irritation and dermal sensitization tests for medical materials and devices.
evidence.evidenceText: It is intended to be the basic document for the selection and conduct of tests enabling evaluation of irritation and dermal sensitization responses relevant to safety of medical materials and devices.

evidence.claim: ISO 10993-10 requires a step-wise approach for irritation and sensitization evaluation.
evidence.evidenceText: This part of ISO 10993 requires a step-wise approach, which shall include one or more of the following: a) characterization of test material... b) literature review... c) in accordance with ISO 10993-2, in vitro tests in preference to in vivo tests shall be considered... d) in vivo animal tests... e) Non-invasive human tests/clinical trials

evidence.claim: For implants or external communicating devices, intracutaneous testing is required for detection of irritation activity.
evidence.evidenceText: For medical devices that are used as implants or external communicating devices, intradermal testing is more relevant in approaching the application and so for detection of irritation activity, intracutaneous testing shall be used as described in 6.4.

evidence.claim: Pretest considerations can lead to a conclusion that irritation or sensitization testing is not necessary.
evidence.evidenceText: It is important to emphasise that pretest considerations may result in the conclusion that testing for irritation and/or sensitization is not necessary.

evidence.claim: Full qualitative chemical constituent data must be established, and relevant quantitative data must be obtained or justified if absent.
evidence.evidenceText: Full qualitative data on the chemical constituents of the material shall be established. Where relevant to biological safety, quantitative data shall also be obtained. If quantitative data are not obtained, the rationale shall be documented and justified.

evidence.claim: The chemical composition of finalized products must be determined according to ISO 10993-18.
evidence.evidenceText: The chemical composition of finalized products shall be determined in accordance with ISO 10993-18.

evidence.claim: In vitro skin irritation testing had been validated for neat chemicals but not for medical device extracts.
evidence.evidenceText: The in vitro test for skin irritation has so far been validated only for neat chemicals and not for medical device extracts. In order to apply these assays for the testing of irritation potential of medical devices, further validation for this specific area is essential.

evidence.claim: Rabbit is the preferred test animal for animal irritation testing.
evidence.evidenceText: An assessment is made of the potential of the material under test to produce dermal irritation in a relevant animal model. The rabbit is the preferred test animal.

evidence.claim: For animal irritation testing, three healthy young adult albino rabbits weighing not less than 2 kg are used.
evidence.evidenceText: Three healthy young adult albino rabbits of either sex from a single strain, weighing not less than 2 kg, shall be used.

evidence.claim: Single-exposure animal irritation observations are recorded at approximately 1, 24, 48, and 72 hours after patch removal.
evidence.evidenceText: For single-exposure tests, record the appearance of each application site at (1 ± 0,1) h, (24 ± 2) h, (48 ± 2) h and (72 ± 2) h following removal of the patches.

evidence.claim: Primary or cumulative irritation index categories in rabbits are negligible, slight, moderate, and severe.
evidence.evidenceText: Table 2 — Primary or cumulative irritation index categories in a rabbit Mean score Response category 0 to 0,4 Negligible 0,5 to 1,9 Slight 2 to 4,9 Moderate 5 to 8 Severe

evidence.claim: For intracutaneous reactivity testing, final test sample score of 1.0 or less meets the requirements.
evidence.evidenceText: The requirements of the test are met if the final test sample score is 1,0 or less.

evidence.claim: Clinical tests for human skin irritation must be performed in accordance with ISO 14155-1 and ISO 14155-2.
evidence.evidenceText: Clinical tests shall be performed in accordance with ISO 14155-1 and ISO 14155-2. Additional specific requirements for clinical tests are described in Annex C.

evidence.claim: Human volunteers for the human skin irritation test must be at least 18 years old, not pregnant, and not breast-feeding.
evidence.evidenceText: The selected human volunteers shall be at least 18 years of age, not pregnant and not breast-feeding.

evidence.claim: At least 30 volunteers must complete the human skin irritation test, with no less than one-third of either sex.
evidence.evidenceText: At least 30 volunteers shall complete the test, with no less than one-third of either sex.

evidence.claim: The LLNA is the preferred assay for testing skin sensitization potential of chemicals.
evidence.evidenceText: The murine Local Lymph Node Assay (LLNA) was internationally accepted for testing single chemicals as a stand-alone alternative to the guinea pig assays, and is now the preferred assay for chemicals.

evidence.claim: In LLNA, a stimulation index of three or more is positive for designating a test sample as a sensitizer.
evidence.evidenceText: An SI of three or more (W 3,0) shall be considered positive for designating a test sample as a sensitizer.

evidence.claim: For a valid LLNA study, the positive control is conducted either concurrently or within the previous six months.
evidence.evidenceText: For a valid study, the positive control shall be conducted either concurrently or within the previous six months.

evidence.claim: The two guinea pig assays used for sensitizing activity are the Buehler assay and the GPMT.
evidence.evidenceText: The two guinea pig assays currently used for the detection of sensitizing activity of chemicals and medical devices are the Buehler assay and the GPMT.

evidence.claim: The GPMT and Buehler test include induction and challenge phases.
evidence.evidenceText: Both assays consist of an induction and challenge phase, thus covering all stages of hypersensitivity.

evidence.claim: A negative test result does not always exclude the possibility of allergic skin reactions.
evidence.evidenceText: A negative test result does not always exclude the possibility that a product might cause allergic skin reactions.

evidence.claim: Positive or negative results should be scrutinized by follow-up and compared with other information sources.
evidence.evidenceText: Both positive and negative test results in any of the assays should be scrutinized by rigorous follow-up in order to minimize the likelihood of false positive or false negative results. The results should be validated by comparison with other sources of information

evidence.claim: Annex A provides normative preparation requirements for materials used in irritation and sensitization testing.
evidence.evidenceText: Annex A (normative) Preparation of materials for irritation/sensitization testing
evidence.pageTarget: Material Preparation for Irritation and Sensitization Testing

evidence.claim: For sterile final products, the test material must be sterilized using the same process before testing.
evidence.evidenceText: If the final product is supplied in a sterile condition, then the test material shall be sterilized using the same process prior to testing.

reviewFlags.reason: Source metadata authoritySignal is FDA, but the document text identifies ISO as the standards authority and contains a Korean license notice for 식품의약품안전평가원; no FDA authority text is present.
No specific product model is identified; the document applies generally to medical devices and constituent materials.
Some mathematical symbols appear degraded in OCR, such as 'pH u 2,0 or W 11,5' and 'SI of W 3,0'; affected threshold claims should be checked against the original PDF before precise wiki display.
Document date and edition are present, but no separate ISO correction, amendment, or later revision status is provided in the source text.
